quote: a workshop that I currently have a small solar system in.
stewart_read: Jody volunteers that he has already self-installed a solar system on his workshop. This is the single strongest qualification signal on the call — a customer who has already committed money and effort to solar is not a skeptic to be converted; he is a buyer looking for the right path. Randy's response ('Okay. Awesome. Sounds good. Perfect.') treats this as a checkbox answer and moves immediately to address verification. The strategic read would be that Randy is deferring the depth conversation to the specialist — but there is no evidence of that intent, and no curiosity hook is planted to bridge to the appointment. The reactive read holds: Randy missed the anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy deliberately kept the interest question brief to maintain call speed on a warm lead, planning to let the specialist develop the depth.
coaching_implication: Show Randy this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask him: 'What did you do with the fact that Jody already has solar on his workshop?' The schema says top setters spend 60+ seconds on interest_in_solar because depth matters more than the answer. A suggested redirect: 'Wait — you already have a system running? What made you go the DIY route on the workshop?' That one question opens the door to bill pain, system sizing curiosity, and a personalized appointment hook — all without scope creep.

quote: We're actually looking at wanting to install this on our garage.
stewart_read: Jody volunteers a specific, considered install preference — the garage, not the house — and explains his reasoning across two turns (metal roof is more accessible; he wants to keep the house roof clean). This is a customer who has done project planning, not just idle browsing. Randy confirms feasibility ('not a problem at all') but does not anchor on the specificity. The appointment pitch later ('a specialist go over this information with you') is generic — it does not tie back to the garage design Jody has been thinking about. A curiosity hook here ('the specialist will actually build the design specifically for your garage setup — metal roof installs have some interesting options') would have personalized the close without scope creep.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy correctly stayed in setter scope by not explaining metal-roof installation details, leaving that for the specialist.
coaching_implication: The strategic read on scope discipline is valid — Randy should not explain the technical details. But there is a gap between 'don't explain the tech' and 'don't use the customer's own stated preference as the appointment hook.' Coach Randy on the difference: acknowledge the specificity, create curiosity about what the specialist will show him for that specific setup, then close. No numbers, no specs — just personalization.

quote: I know at some point I'm gonna have to replace the roof on my house, but, I wanna keep keep it clean right now.
stewart_read: Jody explicitly states he knows a house roof replacement is coming. The schema defines this as a cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned trigger with a specific bridging template and a 'high' revenue impact rating. Randy says 'Sure. Yeah. Not a problem.' and moves to the title question. This is a clean miss — the roofing cross-sell signal is stated plainly, the schema has a defined response, and Randy does not execute it. The reactive read holds without contest.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have judged that Jody's framing ('I wanna keep it clean right now') indicated he was not ready for a roofing conversation and that pushing it would create friction on a warm call.
coaching_implication: Pull up the cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned section with Randy. The schema template does not require Jody to be 'ready' — it routes the signal to the roofing team, not to Randy to sell roofing. The correct move is a single sentence: 'Ion actually does roofing in-house — when you're ready for that, we can have the roofing team take a look so the solar and roof are planned together.' That is not scope creep; it is a routing note. Randy left a documented revenue signal on the table.

stewart_read: Jody confirms his wife is on the title. Randy's response — 'Okay. Yeah. No problem.' — acknowledges the fact and moves on. The schema is explicit: a spouse on the title must be included in the appointment. Randy never asks whether the wife can be on the Monday 10AM call. The appointment is booked for Jody alone. Per protocols.spouse_decision, this is a protocol violation, not a judgment call. The negative example in the schema (SESSION20, Andy excluded) maps directly to this moment.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have read Jody's confident, self-directed engagement as indicating he is the sole decision-maker and the wife's title status is administrative.
coaching_implication: This is the highest structural risk to the sit. The schema coaching note is unambiguous: 'Setters who win this objection lose the deal.' Randy did not even attempt to include the wife — he acknowledged and moved on. In the 1-on-1, walk through the protocols.spouse_decision template. The fix is one sentence at the booking stage: 'Would your wife be able to join us on Monday at 10? Since she's on the title, it's worth having her on so she can ask questions too.' If Jody says she can't make it, that is the moment to find a time that works for both.

quote: With the company. Yeah. So I've looked at With the company? The the possibility of just doing it myself, but I I wanna get some ideas of what would cost that.
stewart_read: Randy asks whether this is Jody's first time looking at solar for the property. Jody's answer is a partial sentence that Randy misreads — he responds 'Someone else did it. Yeah. Definitely, don't blame me there.' as if Jody said a prior company had installed something. Jody actually said he had researched DIY pricing himself. Randy does not probe for what Jody found in that research — competitive pricing expectations, system sizing assumptions, or cost benchmarks Jody may have formed. The verify.prior_designs item exists precisely to surface this competitive context, and Randy's misread means it goes uncaptured.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy's misread was a transcription artifact and he may have understood Jody correctly — but his response ('Someone else did it') does not match any reading of Jody's actual words, so the reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: Flag this in the 1-on-1 as a listening gap, not a technique gap. Randy's response does not match what Jody said. The coaching point: when a customer's answer is unclear, the correct move is a clarifying question ('Sorry — did you mean you looked into doing it yourself, or that another company came out?'), not a confident misread. The handoff brief should note that Jody has self-researched DIY pricing and the closer may encounter cost expectations formed from that research.

quote: About how long does it take?
stewart_read: Jody asks a schema-defined time_constraint protocol question. Randy's response — 'So we typically block off the slot for an hour, just to make sure you guys don't feel rushed. But really just depends on, how many questions you have. So if you don't have a ton of questions, it shouldn't take super long at all.' — is hedged and open-ended. The schema template is 'It's a 30-minute call — we'll get you in and out.' The schema coaching note is explicit: 'Anchor the time confidently. Vagueness = no-show.' Randy's answer introduces uncertainty about duration at the exact moment Jody is evaluating whether to commit to the appointment. The hedge ('really just depends') is the opposite of the confident anchor the protocol requires.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have been trying to set honest expectations to avoid a customer who feels misled if the call runs long — a good-faith move that happens to contradict the schema's no-show-reduction logic.
coaching_implication: This is a direct protocol deviation with a defined template and a defined consequence (higher no-show rate). In the 1-on-1, show Randy the schema template and the coaching note side by side with his actual response. The fix is not complicated: 'It's about 30 minutes — we block an hour so you don't feel rushed, but most people are done in 30.' That is honest, confident, and anchored. The current response leaves Jody uncertain about his Monday morning commitment.

quote: Let me just double check, see if we work with this utility company here, just because I'm not super familiar with them.
stewart_read: Randy discloses unfamiliarity with Guernsey-Muskingum Electric Co-op on the live call and then goes silent for approximately 40 seconds while Jody waits. The disclosure itself is honest, but the execution — a 40-second unnarrated pause — creates a dead-air moment on a warm call. The coaching point is not that Randy should have known the utility; it is that the pause needed narration ('Give me just a second — I want to make sure I have the right service area pulled up for you') and that territory prep for the assigned market would eliminate the disclosure entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy's transparency about not knowing the utility could read as honest and human — a micro-trust signal rather than a competence gap.
coaching_implication: The transparency is not the problem; the unnarrated silence is. Coach Randy on two things: (1) narrate pauses so the customer knows what is happening, and (2) review the utility providers in his assigned territory before his shift so the lookup is not necessary live. The 40-second silence on a warm call is a momentum drain that could have been a 5-second narrated pause.

quote: Yeah. Sounds good. I'll go ahead and get you done for Monday at ten, and then I'll send you a confirmation text with the days I'm in the meeting while I will start with this phone call here.
stewart_read: Randy books the appointment but skips three required button_up elements: (1) the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake is absent; (2) the no-driving/at-computer anchor is absent; (3) the text follow-up promise is mentioned but the content is generic ('confirmation text with the days') rather than personalized per text_after_call requirements. The wife is not included. The booking is real but the button_up is incomplete, which the schema links directly to no-show rate.
alternative_hypothesis: Randy may have judged that Jody's cooperative, low-friction engagement made the full button_up unnecessary — a warm customer who self-initiated does not need the same anchoring as a skeptical one.
coaching_implication: The button_up protocol exists precisely because show rate is not determined by how warm the customer was at booking — it is determined by how committed they feel when Monday morning arrives. Walk Randy through the required elements: the 'is that fair?' handshake, the no-driving/at-computer ask, and the personalized text tieback. On this call, the tieback writes itself: 'Hey Jody, this is Randy with ION Solar. I have you on the calendar for Monday the [date] at 10AM to review the garage solar design for your property in Blue Rock. Looking forward to it — see you then.' That is the difference between a confirmation and a commitment.
